faq:
Frequently Asked Questions

What is the biggest winter risk for chickens in Santa Cruz?

Moisture is the real concern here, not cold. Wet feathers lose their insulating ability and damp coops promote respiratory disease, so focus on draft-proofing lower gaps while keeping upper ventilation open.

Are heat lamps actually dangerous in a coop?

Yes. Coop fires are a leading cause of poultry loss in backyard flocks, and birds that rely on added heat can be shocked badly if a winter storm knocks out power. If you truly need warmth for chicks or injured birds, a radiant heat panel is safer than a lamp.

How cold can fully feathered adult hens handle?

Healthy adult chickens tolerate temperatures well below freezing by fluffing their feathers to trap warm air. Since our coastal lows rarely drop below the mid-30s, most local flocks stay comfortable without any added heat.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much of a chicken's diet can come from scraps?

Scraps should make up no more than about 10 percent of total intake, with commercial feed providing the rest. Think of them as treats, not meal replacements, so your birds still get balanced nutrition.

Why are raw beans so dangerous for poultry?

Raw or dried beans contain a toxin that can be fatal to birds, so they must always be cooked thoroughly first. Other foods to avoid include avocado skin and pits, chocolate, raw potato and green skins, and anything moldy or heavily salted.

What is the best way to feed scraps to a flock?

Scatter scraps around the run so birds forage and spread out rather than fighting over a pile. Remove any uneaten portions daily so leftovers do not attract rats or other predators.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the bloom and why does it matter?

The bloom is a natural protective layer the hen deposits on the shell that seals the pores and keeps bacteria out. Leaving it intact is what allows clean, unwashed eggs to sit safely at room temperature.

How should I clean a lightly soiled egg?

Gently brush off dry dirt with a cloth or fine sandpaper to preserve the bloom. Save full washing for visibly dirty eggs, use warm running water, never cold soaking, and refrigerate those eggs right away.

How can I tell if an egg is still fresh?

Use the water bowl test: fresh eggs sink and lie flat, older eggs stand upright, and any egg that floats should be discarded. Keeping nesting boxes clean with weekly fresh bedding helps produce cleaner eggs in the first place.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why join a community garden instead of growing at home?

Many Santa Cruz yards are foggy or shaded, and a plot offers reliable full sun, good soil, and water access that can be hard to find at home. You also gain neighbors and local growing knowledge that national resources cannot match.

What should I do if there is a waitlist?

Register at more than one garden, keep your contact information current, and respond quickly when a plot opens. Volunteering at work days builds relationships, and offering to share a plot with a friend can move things along.

What can I grow while I wait for a plot?

Containers on a sunny patio handle vegetables, herbs, and berries well, and a yard-sharing arrangement with a neighbor is another option. Raised beds at home (with landlord permission) and school or church garden programs are also worth exploring.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I tell normal molting from a problem?

Molting feathers drop in a predictable pattern from the head and neck down toward the tail, and the skin underneath looks healthy. If you see raw patches, bald spots in odd places, or signs of irritation, look instead for pecking or parasites.

What parasites should I check for in warm weather?

Mites and lice thrive in summer and gather around the vent and under the wings, with northern fowl mites being common along coastal California. Inspect birds at night and treat with a permethrin-based product plus a thorough coop cleaning.

How can I help feathers grow back faster?

New feathers are mostly protein, so boost intake with sunflower seeds or mealworms during the molt. Reducing crowding and boredom, with at least 10 square feet per bird and some enrichment, also limits stress-related feather loss.

Frequently Asked Questions

How many eggs will four to six hens give me?

A small flock of four to six hens usually produces roughly 15 to 30 eggs a week in peak season, or about 250 to 300 eggs per hen in the first year. Output drops by about 10 to 15 percent each year after that.

Why does egg production drop in winter here?

Hens need roughly 14 to 16 hours of light to lay at their peak, so laying slows as days shorten through fall and winter. In Santa Cruz County, laying is most consistent from about April through September.

Does breed choice really change the numbers?

Yes. Production breeds like Leghorns and ISA Browns can approach 300 eggs a year, while heritage and dual-purpose birds such as Plymouth Rocks and Australorps tend to lay around 200 to 250.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the hardest part of housing ducks and chickens together?

Water is the main challenge, since ducks splash constantly and soak the bedding. Placing water stations outside the coop keeps the nighttime sleeping area dry and prevents the ammonia buildup that harms chickens.

How much space do I need for a mixed flock?

UC ANR standards suggest a minimum of about 4 square feet per chicken and 6 square feet per duck inside the coop, which is more room than a chicken-only setup. Extra ventilation near the roofline also helps manage the moisture ducks bring in.

Do the two species need separate sleeping and nesting areas?

Their natural habits work in your favor, since chickens roost on elevated bars while ducks sleep and lay on the ground. Add a low, open nesting area with extra straw on the floor for the ducks, and watch drakes closely because they can injure hens.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why would chickens suddenly avoid a coop they used to like?

Birds instinctively want to roost in an enclosed space at dusk, so refusing usually means something inside is wrong. Red mites that hide by day and feed at night are the most common culprit, followed by trapped heat, poor airflow, or crowding.

How do I check for red mites?

Inspect the roost bars and nearby cracks and crevices after dark with a flashlight, looking for tiny gray or red specks and small blood smears. Treating the birds and cleaning the coop thoroughly usually brings them back inside.

How do I get new birds to use the coop?

New or relocated chickens often need training, so physically place them on the roost at dusk for three to five nights in a row. Make sure there is enough room too, about 4 square feet of floor and 8 to 10 inches of roost bar per standard bird.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the most reliable way to confirm worms?

A fecal float test on a fresh dropping sample, run by a veterinarian, is the surest way to know. It is quick and affordable, and it tells you which parasite you are dealing with before you treat.

What symptoms should make me suspicious?

Watch for weight loss despite a good appetite, pale combs, lower egg production, diarrhea that may contain visible worms, and a hunched, lethargic bird. Roundworms, cecal worms, and capillary worms are the most common in California backyard flocks.

Why are coastal flocks more at risk?

Damp coastal soil helps worm eggs survive, and free-ranging birds pick them up across a wide area. Rotating ground when you can, improving run drainage, removing droppings, and letting in sunlight all help break the cycle.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is an occasional sneeze something to worry about?

Usually not. The most common cause is simple dust irritation from bedding, poor ventilation, or ammonia, and improving airflow often resolves it. If ammonia is noticeable at bird height, do a full bedding change.

When does sneezing signal a real infection?

Be concerned if sneezing comes with bubbles in the corner of the eye, swollen sinuses, rattling breath, or mucus from the nostrils. Those signs point to a respiratory infection that warrants a poultry-experienced veterinarian.

How can I protect the rest of my flock?

Quarantine any new birds for at least 30 days before mixing them in, and isolate sick birds in a clean, warm, well-ventilated space. This matters because some respiratory diseases, such as Mycoplasma gallisepticum, stay with a bird for life even after symptoms fade.

Frequently Asked Questions

Which type is better for a Santa Cruz garden?

Indeterminate (vining) varieties are the better default here, since our mild coastal climate lets them keep fruiting for three to four months longer than determinates. That often means harvests stretching from July into November.

When does a determinate tomato make more sense?

Determinate (bush) types shine for containers, small spaces, and mountain gardens with shorter seasons. Because they ripen most of their fruit in a two to four week window, they are also ideal if you want a concentrated batch for sauce or canning.

What kind of support do indeterminate tomatoes need?

They can reach six feet or taller, so plan for sturdy six-foot stakes or heavy-gauge cages set in place before planting. Flimsy store-bought cages tend to collapse under the weight of a full vining plant.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the single most effective step against rats?

Bring the feeders inside each evening before dark. Rats feed mainly at night, so removing their overnight food source cuts activity dramatically.

How should I store feed so rats cannot reach it?

Keep feed in metal containers with tight-fitting lids, since rats eventually chew through plastic bins. Store those containers in a shed or garage rather than next to the coop.

Is rat poison safe to use around a flock?

No. Poison baits carry a serious risk of secondary poisoning for your chickens and local wildlife. Use snap traps inside secured stations placed along walls and known rat routes instead.

Frequently Asked Questions

When can I find chicks at local feed stores?

Feed stores in the area typically stock chicks and ducklings from late February through May, with the best selection in March and April. Popular breeds sell out fast, so call ahead and check two or three stores.

What should I ask about before buying chickens?

Look for NPIP-certified sources, which means the birds have been tested for disease, and ask whether chicks were vaccinated for Marek's disease, which UC Davis recommends for backyard flocks. For unvaccinated chicks, medicated starter feed is a good choice.

Are ducklings harder to find than chicks?

Yes, local duckling availability is limited and often restricted to common breeds. For specific varieties you can order from a California waterfowl hatchery or a mail-order hatchery that ships day-old birds reliably to the area.

Frequently Asked Questions

Are roosters allowed in the City of Santa Cruz?

No. Hens are generally permitted on residential property without a permit, but roosters are prohibited within city limits. Most neighbor complaints come down to noise, which is almost entirely a rooster issue, not a hen one.

What should I check before getting chickens?

Rules vary by jurisdiction, so contact your local planning department to confirm setback and zoning requirements for your address. Also review any rental lease or HOA rules, since those private restrictions can override what the city allows.

How do I protect a backyard flock from local predators?

Use hardware cloth rather than chicken wire, which predators can tear through or reach into. Plan for the area's raccoons, foxes, coyotes, bobcats, and hawks, and keep the coop clean so it does not attract rats and flies.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the best time to start chicks here?

Late March through mid-May is the ideal window for Santa Cruz County. Starting in early April lets chicks be fully feathered and ready for outdoor living by late May, when nights are reliably mild.

What brooder temperature do chicks need?

Chicks need about 95 degrees in their first week, then roughly 5 degrees less each week until they are fully feathered around six weeks old. Have the brooder set up and warmed at least 24 hours before the chicks arrive.

What supplies do I need before the chicks come home?

Set up pine shavings, a heat source, clean water with a way to prevent drowning, and chick starter feed. Medicated starter is recommended if your chicks were not vaccinated.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much water does drip irrigation actually save?

Drip systems use roughly 30 to 70 percent less water than sprinklers or hand watering because the water goes straight to the roots. Keeping foliage dry also reduces disease and encourages deeper, healthier roots.

How should I set my schedule for my part of the county?

It depends on your microclimate. Coastal gardens often need watering only every other day for about 25 to 35 minutes thanks to the fog, while sunnier mountain and Watsonville sandy-soil gardens usually need a daily run of 30 to 45 minutes.

What maintenance keeps a drip system working well?

Clean the filter about once a month and flush the lines at the end of the season to clear sediment. With that simple care, components typically last anywhere from 5 to 15 years.
